Keywords: fitts law touch target size distance placement
Core Principle: Time to acquire a target is a function of its distance and size — smaller and farther = harder and slower
Mobile Application: Place primary actions in thumb zone (bottom 60% screen); make CTAs larger than secondary buttons; group related actions together to reduce travel distance
Design Pattern: Large primary CTA at bottom; secondary button above or inline; delete/destructive actions far from confirm to prevent mistaps
Do: Make primary CTA button at least 44pt tall and full-width or dominant width; place in bottom thumb zone; destructive actions far from confirm
Don't: Small icon buttons without tap area expansion; placing primary action at top right (hardest to reach one-handed); equal size for primary/secondary buttons
Research Source: Laws of UX / Fitts (1954) / MIT Touch Lab

Keywords: hicks law choices decision time navigation options menu
Core Principle: Decision time increases with the number and complexity of choices — more options = slower decision
Mobile Application: Limit bottom tab bar to 3-5 items; use progressive disclosure for settings; limit paywall plans to 2-3 tiers; don't show full action sheets as default state
Design Pattern: Tab bar with max 5 items; paywall with 2-3 plans; onboarding with 2-3 answer options per question; collapsed settings with drill-down
Do: Limit navigation to 5 items per level; highlight recommended option; use progressive onboarding not full feature reveal on day 1
Don't: Settings screen listing all 40 options in one flat list; paywall with 5 pricing tiers all equal weight; tab bar with 7 tabs

Keywords: jakobs law mental model familiarity convention platform
Core Principle: Users spend most time in other apps — they expect yours to work like those apps
Mobile Application: Follow iOS HIG and Material Design 3 conventions for navigation patterns; don't reinvent tab bars hamburger icons or back gestures; use platform-standard icons (SF Symbols on iOS Icons on Android)
Design Pattern: Bottom tab bar on iOS (not top); back button top-left iOS; FAB bottom-right Android; swipe-back on iOS; predictive-back on Android 13+
Do: Use platform-standard navigation patterns; use system icons before custom ones; match share/edit/delete interactions to platform defaults
Don't: Custom swipe direction (horizontal vs vertical) inconsistent with platform; inverted back gesture; tab bar at top on iOS phone

Keywords: goal gradient progress bar completion steps motivation
Core Principle: The tendency to approach a goal increases as users get closer to completing it — show progress to motivate completion
Mobile Application: Show step indicators in onboarding (Step 2 of 4); progress bars in multi-step forms; completion percentage in profiles; highlight how close user is to next reward tier
Design Pattern: Onboarding step indicator; profile completeness bar; loyalty points progress; 'You're 80% done – add one more thing' state
Do: Show step X of Y in any multi-step flow; display progress bars that update in real time; show 'almost there' states near completion
Don't: Multi-step onboarding with no progress indicator; profile with no completion signal; checkout with unknown number of steps remaining
Research Source: Laws of UX / Hull (1932) / Kivetz et al. (2006)

Keywords: peak end rule memorable experience aha moment delight
Core Principle: People judge experiences by their peak moment and end moment — not the average of all steps
Mobile Application: Design a memorable 'wow' moment (aha moment) in onboarding; make the completion/success state delightful (confetti sound haptic); don't let the app end on an error screen
Design Pattern: Lottie confetti on first task completion; success haptic + microinteraction on account creation; celebratory empty state on goal achievement
Do: Create a peak moment after first data creation or first value delivery; give the success/completion state visual delight; always end redirected flows on a positive state
Don't: Plain success toast notification with no delight; crash without recovery; ending checkout flow on a dry confirmation screen
Research Source: Laws of UX / Kahneman et al. (1993)

Keywords: doherty threshold response time 400ms performance loading feedback
Core Principle: Productivity soars when system responds in under 400ms — longer waits break flow and increase abandonment
Mobile Application: Show feedback within 400ms of any tap; optimistic UI updates (update state before server confirms); skeleton screens instead of spinners for >300ms loads; perceive-fast strategies
Design Pattern: Optimistic tap response (button state changes <16ms); skeleton shimmer for 300ms+ loads; progress bar for operations >2s; suppress spinner for <300ms operations
Do: Respond to any tap within 400ms minimum with visual feedback; use optimistic UI for actions likely to succeed; show progress for operations >2s
Don't: No visual feedback on button tap; full-page loading spinner that blocks all content; no response to network retry after error

Keywords: millers law 7 chunks cognitive load working memory
Core Principle: Average person can hold 7±2 items in working memory — design for cognitive limits by chunking
Mobile Application: Group bottom nav to max 5 items; limit list filter chips to 5-7 visible; break onboarding into max 4-5 questions; chunk settings into logical groups max 7 items per group
Design Pattern: Section headers in long settings lists; grouped form fields; onboarding quiz with 3-5 questions not 15; stagger tips one at a time
Do: Chunk navigation options into logical groups; limit visible choices to 5-7 at a time; group form fields with clear section labels
Don't: Settings with 30 ungrouped flat options; filter dropdown with 25 checkboxes all visible; coach mark showing 12 tips at once

Keywords: aesthetic usability effect beautiful design perceived easier trust
Core Principle: Users perceive aesthetically pleasing designs as more usable and more trustworthy — beauty signals quality
Mobile Application: Polished UI increases trust (critical for apps handling personal data); invest in premium visual design; small visual details (icon consistency corner radius shadows) increase perceived reliability
Design Pattern: Premium onboarding with consistent illustration style; well-crafted empty state illustrations; consistent color usage across all screens
Do: Use consistent visual language throughout (same icon style corner radius spacing system); invest in onboarding visual quality; polish error states not just success states
Don't: Mismatched icon styles (filled vs outline mixed); inconsistent border radius (some 4dp some 16dp same screen); cluttered low-contrast layouts
Research Source: Laws of UX / Tractinsky et al. (2000)

Keywords: zeigarnik effect incomplete tasks progress streaks retention
Core Principle: People remember uncompleted tasks better than completed ones — use to drive engagement and return visits
Mobile Application: Streaks and daily goals leverage Zeigarnik Effect — incomplete streak is memorable and motivates return; partially completed profile drives completion; notifications about incomplete actions
Design Pattern: Daily streak counter; 'You're 1 action away from your goal today'; unread badge on tab; incomplete profile percentage; push notification 'You haven't logged today'
Do: Use streak mechanics for daily habits; show incomplete progress indicators; send contextual re-engagement at incomplete action moment
Don't: Reset streaks without warning; show 100% completion immediately on first open (nothing to complete); no persistence of in-progress states across sessions

Keywords: von restorff isolation effect highlight standout visual emphasis
Core Principle: When multiple similar objects are present, the one that differs from the rest is most likely to be remembered
Mobile Application: Highlight primary CTA with color when all others are outline/ghost; use accent color for single most important UI element; recommended plan in paywall should have distinct visual treatment
Design Pattern: Filled primary button vs outline secondary; highlighted recommended paywall tier (badge 'Most Popular'); accent-colored notification badge on single tab
Do: Use accent color on only ONE primary action per screen; visually distinguish the recommended option in any multi-choice UI; use badge/indicator selectively
Don't: All buttons same visual weight; paywall with identical card style for all 3 plans; every nav item with a badge (defeats the purpose)

Keywords: serial position effect first last middle recall navigation menu
Core Principle: Users best remember the first and last items in a series — middle items are forgotten more often
Mobile Application: Put most important navigation items first and last in tab bar; most important setting at top of list; critical action at bottom of action sheet (last = most remembered); hide promotional items in middle
Design Pattern: Primary tab (Home) at position 1; secondary tab (Profile/Settings) at position last; most common action in action sheet at top and bottom; promotional item in middle of list
Do: Place most accessed navigation destinations at first and last positions; put critical settings at top of section; most important action sheet option at top
Don't: Home at position 3 of 5; putting promotional tab first; burying most-used feature in the middle of list

Keywords: cognitive load working memory reduce simplify screen
Core Principle: Don't overload users' working memory — the less users have to remember, the more likely they are to complete the task
Mobile Application: Reduce form fields to minimum required; show persistent visible labels on inputs (not placeholder-only); don't make users remember info across screens; autocomplete saves re-entry
Design Pattern: Persistent text field labels; address autocomplete; remembered payment method; pre-fill known values from earlier onboarding answers; 1-2 primary actions per screen
Do: Surface what users need at the moment they need it; don't make users remember from previous screen; reduce choices on any single action screen
Don't: Form with no visible field labels after focus; checkout requiring re-entry of known info; 6+ actions in bottom action sheet; cluttered dashboard with 30 cards

Keywords: law of proximity grouping related elements visual nearness
Core Principle: Elements placed near each other are perceived as related — use spacing to communicate relationships
Mobile Application: Group related form fields; price and 'Subscribe' CTA within same visual group; label directly adjacent to its input; destructive button spaced far from confirm
Design Pattern: 8pt gap within a group; 24pt gap between groups; price+CTA+terms in same card; delete button at bottom with extra margin from edit/save
Do: Use consistent large gap between unrelated UI groups; small consistent gap within related groups; the label of a toggle should be directly adjacent
Don't: Delete button at same spacing as edit button; form groups with same gap as between groups; toggle with label 20pt away

Keywords: law of common region grouping card container background
Core Principle: Elements within the same visual container are perceived as belonging together
Mobile Application: Use cards to group related content (post = image+text+actions+author in one card); section headers define a region; modal sheets create a focused region
Design Pattern: Card containing all post elements; settings grouped in visual container; bottom sheet as modal region; tab bar as own region separated by divider
Do: Use explicit visual boundaries (cards dividers backgrounds) to group related elements; don't use implicit grouping alone for critical relationships
Don't: Flat list with no grouping where context matters; action buttons floating without clear association to their content; mixed unrelated elements in same card

Keywords: paradox active user tutorial skip documentation learn
Core Principle: Users start using software immediately without reading instructions — they learn by doing, not by reading
Mobile Application: Don't rely on overlays or tutorials for core feature understanding — design should be self-explanatory; coach marks must be contextual and minimal (1 at a time); trigger tips at point-of-need not on launch
Design Pattern: Single contextual coach mark at moment of first relevant action; tooltip on first long-press; empty state with action CTA instead of instructions
Do: Make the primary action obvious without instructions; show only one coach mark at a time; trigger tips contextually not on launch; test without any tutorial
Don't: Full-screen tutorial on first launch requiring reading; 10-step coach mark sequence; long text instructions before any interaction
